title: Dog Scratching Ears and Shaking Head: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog shaking their head? Learn about ear infections, why this is a medium-urgency concern, and how photos help your vet diagnose the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: Frequent head shaking or ear scratching usually indicates a painful ear infection or irritation that requires a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if your dog shows a head tilt, loss of balance, or a severely swollen ear flap.

Why is my dog shaking their head or scratching their ears?

  • It is a primary sign of ear discomfort, inflammation, or pain.
  • Most frequently associated with otitis externa (infection caused by yeast, bacteria, or ear mites).
  • Can be caused by allergies or a foreign object, such as a grass seed, trapped in the canal.
  • May result in an ear hematoma caused by the trauma of shaking.

Is head shaking and ear scratching in dogs an emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Ear conditions are extremely painful and can lead to ruptured eardrums or permanent hearing loss if left untreated.
  • Seek immediate care if: Your dog has a head tilt, loss of balance, or if the ear flap appears swollen and squishy like a balloon.

How does taking a photo help triage my dog's ear issue?

  • Provides visual information on the color and consistency of discharge, such as dark brown waxy buildup or yellow pus.
  • Shows the veterinary team the degree of redness and swelling inside the ear flap.
  • Helps the clinic determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.
  • Assists the team in identifying which diagnostic tools may be required upon your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, head shaking and pruritus are common historical findings associated with ear problems. Initial physical examination should assess pain severity through gentle palpation; sedation may be required for painful ears before further diagnostics. Palpation and manipulation of the ear canal and pinna can reveal swelling, pruritus, fibrosis, or calcification, guiding the need for advanced imaging. Examination of the external ear should note erythema, edema, crusts, scales, ulcers, lichenification, hyperpigmentation, or exudate. Arthropod bites, such as from ticks, can cause dermatitis of the pinnae, leading to head shaking, head rubbing, or drooped pinnae. Treatment involves mechanical tick removal and appropriate acaricides, along with addressing secondary bacterial or yeast otitis externa.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 528)

faqs.question: Can I use over-the-counter human ear drops for my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never put human medications or cleaners in a dog's ear without professional guidance, as some ingredients can be toxic if the eardrum is ruptured.

faqs.question: What if my dog's ear flap is swollen?
faqs.answer: A swollen ear flap is likely an aural hematoma, caused by broken blood vessels from intense shaking. This requires veterinary intervention to drain the fluid and address the underlying infection.

faqs.question: How can I prevent ear infections in the future?
faqs.answer: Regularly cleaning your dog's ears with a vet-approved cleanser, especially after swimming or bathing, can help prevent moisture buildup and infections.
